events.text: The patient is a 52-year-old female named Margaret Chen. She is a never-smoker with no significant alcohol use. She works as a high school music teacher. She has no family history of head and neck cancer. She is of Chinese-American ethnicity. Past medical history is notable for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine and seasonal allergic rhinitis.

At age 52 years, the patient received a diagnosis of Stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition p16+ classification) SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, HPV-POSITIVE (p16+) of the BASE OF TONGUE (ICD-O site code C01.9, histology code 8070/3). The primary tumor measured 3.1 cm in greatest dimension at the base of tongue, right-sided, without extension beyond the oropharynx. A single ipsilateral right level II cervical lymph node measuring 2.4 cm was identified, without extranodal extension. No distant metastases were identified. Relevant biomarkers included p16 immunohistochemistry: STRONGLY POSITIVE (3+, >75% nuclear and cytoplasmic staining); HPV in situ hybridization: POSITIVE for high-risk HPV (type 16); Tumor grade: Moderately differentiated. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY via direct laryngoscopy and biopsy. The patient presented with a 3-month history of right-sided throat discomfort and mild odynophagia, initially attributed to reflux. Indirect laryngoscopy by ENT demonstrated a right base of tongue mass. Performance status ECOG 0.

At age 52 years, the patient had a pathology result of type ANATOMIC PATHOLOGY from a right base of tongue biopsy. Gross description: Multiple tan-pink irregular tissue fragments aggregating 1.2 cm. Microscopic findings: Invasive moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with nested and sheet-like growth pattern. Tumor cells have abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and pleomorphic nuclei with prominent nucleoli. Lymphovascular invasion: Not identified. Perineural invasion: Not identified. Margins: Not applicable (biopsy specimen). Immunohistochemistry: p16 (CINtec) POSITIVE, strong diffuse nuclear and cytoplasmic staining in >75% of tumor cells. HPV RNA ISH (RNAscope): POSITIVE, consistent with transcriptionally active HPV. Final diagnosis: Squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-associated (p16 positive), base of tongue.

At age 52 years, the patient had a CT NECK WITH CONTRAST (diagnostic), which showed a 3.1 cm heterogeneously enhancing mass centered at the right base of tongue with involvement of the right glossoepiglottic fold. No extension into the vallecula, epiglottis, or pre-epiglottic space. A right level IIA cervical lymph node measuring 2.4 x 1.8 cm with internal heterogeneity suspicious for nodal metastasis. No radiographic evidence of extranodal extension. No contralateral adenopathy. Airway patent. Cancer present, locoregional disease only.

At age 52 years, the patient had a PET/CT (FDG) WHOLE BODY for staging, which showed FDG-avid right base of tongue primary tumor (SUVmax 12.4, 3.1 cm). FDG-avid right level IIA lymph node (SUVmax 8.7, 2.4 cm). No FDG-avid lymph nodes below the clavicles. No FDG-avid pulmonary nodules. No hepatic, osseous, or other distant metastatic disease identified. Impression: Locoregional HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, stage III (T2N1M0). No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Cancer present, locoregional only.

At age 52 years, the patient had an MRI OROPHARYNX/NECK WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST for treatment planning, which showed a 3.0 cm T2-hyperintense, heterogeneously enhancing right base of tongue mass. Involvement of the right glossoepiglottic fold. No tongue base midline crossing. No pre-epiglottic space involvement. No involvement of the mandible, pterygoid musculature, or parapharyngeal space. Right level IIA lymph node 2.4 cm with irregular enhancement, no frank extranodal extension. Precise tumor volume delineation performed for IMRT planning. Cancer present.

At age 52 years, the patient had next generation sequencing performed for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the right base of tongue biopsy (primary tumor). Tumor mutational burden: 5 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: MSS (microsatellite stable). Actionable alterations: PIK3CA p.E545K mutation (variant allele frequency 28%, potentially actionable - PI3K pathway). Comutations of uncertain/low significance: FAT1 p.R1837* mutation (VAF 18%); KMT2D p.Q1389fs mutation (VAF 22%); NOTCH1 p.P2415fs mutation (VAF 15%); EP300 p.R240H mutation (VAF 12%). Copy number alterations: CCND1 focal gain (chromosome 11q13); FGFR1 low-level gain. No high-level amplifications identified. No CDKN2A deletion. No TP53 mutation (consistent with HPV-driven carcinogenesis; TP53 wild-type). No KRAS, HRAS, or NRAS mutations. No actionable fusions identified. HPV integration: Evidence of HPV-16 genomic integration detected by sequencing. PD-L1 expression (concurrent IHC): CPS 12 (Combined Positive Score). EGFR copy number: Polysomy, no high-level amplification. Note: TP53 wild-type status and PIK3CA E545K mutation are consistent with HPV-positive OPSCC genomic profile.

At age 52 years, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board evaluation at the Head and Neck Oncology program. Attendance included radiation oncology, medical oncology, head and neck surgery, speech-language pathology, nutrition/dietetics, and dental oncology. Discussion: Patient presents with T2N1M0, p16-positive oropharyngeal SCC of the right base of tongue. Given HPV-positive status, excellent performance status (ECOG 0), and absence of adverse pathologic features, definitive concurrent chemoradiation with cisplatin was recommended as the standard of care approach. Primary surgical resection via TORS was discussed but not recommended given the node-positive disease and anticipated morbidity. De-escalation trial enrollment (e.g., lower-dose radiation to 60 Gy instead of standard 70 Gy) was considered but patient was not enrolled in a formal trial. Dental evaluation completed; one molar extraction required prior to radiation. Pre-treatment swallowing evaluation and baseline audiogram completed. PEG tube placement discussed as prophylactic option vs. as-needed; decision deferred with plan to reassess during treatment. Cancer present, no response assessment yet.

At age 52 years, the patient underwent pre-radiation DENTAL EXTRACTION of tooth #19 (lower left first molar) with evidence of significant periapical disease and deemed high risk for osteoradionecrosis if left in situ during radiation. Procedure performed by oral and maxillofacial surgery under local anesthesia without complication. Socket healing confirmed at 14-day follow-up prior to radiation start. A custom oral stent/tongue depressor device was also fabricated for radiation treatment positioning.

At age 52 years, the patient began definitive INTENSITY-MODULATED RADIATION THERAPY (IMRT) to the oropharynx and bilateral neck using a simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique. Planning target volumes: PTV_High (primary tumor and involved right level IIA node): 70 Gy in 35 fractions (2.0 Gy/fraction); PTV_Intermediate (high-risk elective bilateral neck, levels II-IV): 59.5 Gy in 35 fractions; PTV_Low (low-risk elective neck): 56 Gy in 35 fractions. Treatment delivered Monday through Friday, 5 days per week, over 7 weeks. Image guidance: daily cone-beam CT. Mean parotid dose: right 28 Gy, left 24 Gy (IMRT sparing technique employed). Concurrent systemic therapy: cisplatin 100 mg/m2 IV every 3 weeks (planned x2 cycles during radiation). Radiation oncologist: Dr. Jennifer Park. Simulation date preceded treatment start by 10 days.

At age 52 years, the patient received cycle 1 of concurrent CISPLATIN 100 mg/m2 IV (actual dose 165 mg given BSA 1.65 m2) as part of definitive chemoradiation for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC. Pre-medications included ondansetron, dexamethasone, and aggressive IV hydration (2L NS). Granisetron patch applied for outpatient nausea prophylaxis. Baseline creatinine 0.8 mg/dL; GFR estimated at 88 mL/min. Concurrent with IMRT week 1. Administered without complication on day 1 of radiation. Planned 3-week cycles x2.

At age 52 years (week 3 of chemoradiation), the patient developed GRADE 2 MUCOSITIS (CTCAE v5.0) involving the oropharynx and base of tongue. Symptoms included moderate pain rated 5/10, requiring scheduled acetaminophen and intermittent oxycodone. Oral rinses (magic mouthwash, sodium bicarbonate, and saline) initiated. Mild dysphagia to solids noted. Weight loss of 3 lbs from baseline (2% body weight). Nutritional counseling reinforced with high-calorie oral supplements. Patient still tolerating adequate oral intake. Radiation continued without interruption.

At age 52 years, the patient received cycle 2 of concurrent CISPLATIN 100 mg/m2 IV (day 22 of radiation, week 4 of IMRT). Creatinine stable at 0.9 mg/dL; GFR 82 mL/min (acceptable, full dose administered). Hearing screen: high-frequency audiogram showed no significant change from baseline. Nausea grade 1, managed with scheduled ondansetron. Patient noted increasing odynophagia and difficulty with solid foods. Dietitian engaged for nutritional monitoring. Weight now 4.5 lbs below baseline (3% total body weight loss). Administered with full dose without dose reduction.

At age 52 years (week 5 of chemoradiation), the patient developed GRADE 3 MUCOSITIS (CTCAE v5.0). Findings included confluent pseudomembranous mucositis involving the entire oropharynx, soft palate, and posterior tongue. Pain rated 8/10 despite scheduled extended-release oxycodone and breakthrough immediate-release oxycodone. Severe odynophagia with near-inability to swallow liquids. Weight loss now 9 lbs from baseline (6% total body weight). Oral intake insufficient to meet nutritional needs (estimated <25% of daily caloric requirements). PERCUTANEOUS ENDOSCOPIC GASTROSTOMY (PEG) TUBE PLACED by gastroenterology at this time for nutritional support. Patient begun on tube feeding with Osmolite 1.5 at goal rate of 80 mL/hr x 14 hours overnight. Dermatitis grade 2 of the bilateral neck skin also noted (dry desquamation). Continued radiation without treatment break.

At age 52 years (week 5 of chemoradiation), the patient underwent PERCUTANEOUS ENDOSCOPIC GASTROSTOMY (PEG) TUBE PLACEMENT performed by gastroenterology under conscious sedation. Indication: Grade 3 radiation-induced mucositis with inability to maintain adequate oral caloric intake. Procedure performed without complications. 20 Fr PEG tube placed in appropriate position confirmed fluoroscopically. Tube feeds initiated at 6 hours post-procedure. Patient and family educated on PEG tube care, flushing, and administration of tube feeds and medications.

At age 52 years (week 6-7 of chemoradiation), the patient developed GRADE 2 RADIATION DERMATITIS involving bilateral anterior and posterior neck skin with moist desquamation in the skin folds bilaterally. Managed with aquaphor ointment, non-adherent dressings, and wound care nursing visits. Additionally, GRADE 1 XEROSTOMIA developed (persistent dry mouth) expected with parotid gland irradiation. Patient also developed GRADE 2 FATIGUE, managed with activity pacing and energy conservation strategies. No febrile neutropenia or hospital admission required during treatment. Radiation completed on schedule without any treatment interruptions. Total treatment time: 7 weeks (35 fractions completed). Cisplatin: 2 of 2 planned cycles completed at full dose.

At age 52 years, the patient completed definitive concurrent chemoradiation (70 Gy IMRT + cisplatin x2 cycles) for HPV-positive right base of tongue SCC, T2N1M0. End-of-treatment assessment: Patient has completed full planned course of treatment. Grade 3 mucositis present at treatment completion but improving. PEG tube in place and being used exclusively for nutrition. Weight loss totaling 11 lbs (7.5% of baseline body weight) over course of treatment. Performance status ECOG 2 at end of treatment (limited due to mucositis, fatigue, and dysphagia). Plan: Serial oncologic surveillance imaging at 8-12 weeks post-treatment per NCCN guidelines (PET/CT preferred). Speech-language pathology for dysphagia rehabilitation. Continue PEG tube until adequate oral intake restored. Nephrology and audiology follow-up per cisplatin exposure. Return to clinic in 2 weeks for first post-treatment assessment.

At age 52 years (6 weeks post-chemoradiation completion), the patient had an oncologist office visit. Interval improvement noted: mucositis grade 1 (mild residual erythema and tenderness). Pain now managed with scheduled acetaminophen only, narcotic discontinued. Patient beginning oral trials per speech-language pathology guidance - tolerating pureed foods and thin liquids. PEG tube still in place; approximately 50% of calories by tube, 50% oral. Weight stabilized, net loss 9 lbs from pre-treatment baseline. ECOG performance status 1. Xerostomia persisting, grade 1-2, using artificial saliva spray and sipping water frequently. Neck skin healed, residual hyperpigmentation. Cancer status: clinical exam shows marked regression of right base of tongue mass; no palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. Response to therapy: apparent response. Awaiting 12-week post-treatment PET/CT for definitive response assessment.

At age 52 years (12 weeks post-completion of chemoradiation), the patient had a PET/CT (FDG) WHOLE BODY for post-treatment response assessment. Findings: No FDG-avid residual primary tumor at the right base of tongue (previously SUVmax 12.4; now no focal uptake above background, SUVmax 1.8 at site of primary). No FDG-avid cervical lymphadenopathy identified bilaterally. No new FDG-avid lesions in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis. Mild diffuse FDG uptake in the oropharynx consistent with post-radiation inflammation. No evidence of distant metastatic disease. IMPRESSION: COMPLETE METABOLIC RESPONSE to definitive chemoradiation. No evidence of residual or recurrent locoregional disease. No distant metastases. Cancer absent.

At age 52 years (12 weeks post-chemoradiation), the patient had an oncologist office visit for post-treatment response assessment. PET/CT reviewed: COMPLETE RESPONSE confirmed. Clinical examination: no visible or palpable residual tumor at base of tongue on flexible nasopharyngoscopy; no palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. Patient counseled extensively on the excellent prognosis associated with HPV-positive OPSCC complete response after definitive chemoradiation (5-year OS >85% in this subgroup). Discussion of de-escalation: Patient not enrolled in adjuvant de-escalation trial. No adjuvant systemic therapy planned (standard of care is surveillance after complete response). Plan: Continue NCCN-guided surveillance per HPV-positive OPSCC protocol (H&P every 1-3 months for years 1-2, then every 2-6 months years 3-5, then annually after year 5). Consider PEG tube removal when oral intake adequate. Dental and speech-language pathology follow-up ongoing. Cancer absent, complete response to therapy.

At age 52 years (approximately 5 months post-chemoradiation completion), the patient underwent PERCUTANEOUS ENDOSCOPIC GASTROSTOMY (PEG) TUBE REMOVAL. Indication: Adequate oral caloric intake re-established; patient tolerating soft mechanical diet and thin liquids, meeting >85% of daily caloric needs orally. Weight recovering toward baseline (net loss now only 4 lbs from pre-treatment baseline). Procedure performed by gastroenterology in clinic by cutting and removing external bumper; internal bumper allowed to pass naturally. No complications. Patient to continue soft diet and follow up with speech-language pathology for ongoing dysphagia rehabilitation.

At age 52 years (6 months post-chemoradiation), the patient had a surveillance visit with medical oncology. The patient reports returning to work part-time as a music teacher. Diet advancing - tolerating soft mechanical diet, working toward regular diet. Xerostomia persisting (grade 1), using saliva substitutes and pilocarpine 5 mg TID with partial benefit. PEG tube removed 1 month ago. Weight within 2 lbs of pre-treatment baseline. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy: no visible tumor at base of tongue, mucosa healing well. No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. Performance status ECOG 0. Cancer absent. No evidence of disease recurrence. Thyroid function: TSH mildly elevated at 5.8 mIU/L (likely radiation-induced hypothyroidism); levothyroxine dose increased. Reinforce sun protection for neck skin. Next surveillance imaging (CT neck with contrast) at 12 months post-treatment.

At age 53 years (12 months post-chemoradiation completion), the patient had a CT NECK WITH CONTRAST for surveillance. Findings: Post-radiation changes in the oropharynx including mucosal thickening and soft tissue stranding in the right base of tongue region, consistent with expected post-treatment sequelae. No discrete mass lesion or abnormal enhancement to suggest residual or recurrent primary tumor. Right level IIA region: small residual 0.8 cm lymph node with homogeneous enhancement, decreased from 2.4 cm at diagnosis, consistent with complete treatment response and post-treatment fibrosis. No new or enlarging cervical lymphadenopathy. No evidence of distant disease on chest/upper abdomen included cuts. IMPRESSION: No evidence of disease recurrence at 12-month post-treatment surveillance. Cancer absent.

At age 53 years (12 months post-chemoradiation), the patient had a surveillance visit. CT neck reviewed - no evidence of recurrence. Patient reports feeling well overall, returned to full-time teaching. Diet: tolerating regular diet with some limitations (avoids very dry/chewy foods due to xerostomia). Xerostomia grade 1 persisting, manageable. No significant trismus. Neck range of motion preserved with regular physical therapy exercises. No dysphagia at rest; mild effort dysphagia with large food boluses managed behaviorally. Audiogram: mild high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss (4-8 kHz) bilaterally, attributed to cisplatin, not requiring hearing aids at this time. Endocrinology: thyroid function normalized on adjusted levothyroxine dose. Cancer absent. Surveillance: continue per NCCN guidelines. Next visit in 3 months; imaging annually or as clinically indicated. Patient counseled on signs/symptoms of recurrence and to report promptly.

At age 53 years (18 months post-chemoradiation completion), the patient had a CT NECK WITH CONTRAST for surveillance. Findings: Stable post-radiation changes in the oropharynx. No new mass, abnormal enhancement, or FDG-avid lesion (PET not performed at this interval per clinical discretion). Bilateral cervical lymph nodes: no pathologically enlarged lymph nodes. Previously noted right level IIA lymph node now 0.6 cm, stable to decreased. No new chest, upper abdominal, or neck findings. IMPRESSION: No evidence of disease recurrence at 18-month post-treatment surveillance. Continued post-radiation changes stable. Cancer absent.

At age 53 years (18 months post-chemoradiation), the patient had a surveillance visit with medical oncology and radiation oncology co-management. No evidence of recurrence on latest imaging. Patient reports excellent quality of life. Xerostomia stable grade 1. No pain. No dysphagia with regular diet. Returned to full professional and social activities. Physical examination: Flexible nasopharyngoscopy by ENT demonstrates well-healed mucosa at the right base of tongue, no masses, no concerning lesions. Bilateral neck without adenopathy. Cancer absent. Late effects discussion: Patient counseled on risk of radiation-induced hypothyroidism (already managed), risk of carotid stenosis with ongoing surveillance, dental hygiene with fluoride trays daily. Ongoing monitoring of hearing recommended annually. No evidence of disease. Continue surveillance per NCCN HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC guidelines.

At age 54 years (24 months post-chemoradiation), the patient had a 2-year surveillance visit. The patient remains well with no symptoms of disease recurrence. No new lymphadenopathy, no throat pain, no unexplained weight loss, no new neck masses. CT neck with contrast obtained and reviewed: no evidence of recurrent or new disease (stable post-radiation changes, no new lesions). Performance status ECOG 0. The patient is counseled that completing 2 years of disease-free follow-up represents a very favorable milestone for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC and that 5-year survival rates exceed 85% for her clinical subgroup. The patient reports interest in reducing surveillance visit frequency. Surveillance intervals adjusted per NCCN: visits every 2-6 months going forward (years 3-5). Patient counseled to report immediately if new neck mass, hemoptysis, or significant unexplained throat pain develops. Cancer absent. No evidence of disease at 24-month landmark.

documents.text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY - SURVEILLANCE VISIT
Head and Neck Oncology Program

ATTENDING: [Medical Oncology Attending]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine post-treatment surveillance visit. Patient with HPV-positive (p16+) right base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma, T2N1M0, status post definitive concurrent chemoradiation, here for 6-month post-treatment follow-up.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Chen is a 52-year-old Chinese-American never-smoker and high school music teacher who was diagnosed approximately 8 months ago with Stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition) HPV-positive squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue. She underwent definitive concurrent chemoradiation: intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) using a simultaneous integrated boost technique delivering 70 Gy in 35 fractions to the high-dose planning target volume (primary tumor and involved right level IIA node), with concurrent cis 100 mg/m2 IV on days 1 and 22. She completed the full planned 35-fraction course without treatment interruptions, receiving both planned cycles of cis at full dose.

Her treatment course was notable for grade 3 radiation mucositis during weeks 5-7, which required percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube placement for nutritional support. She also experienced grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the bilateral neck, grade 1-2 xerostomia, and grade 2 fatigue. Post-treatment PET/CT obtained at approximately 12 weeks post-completion demonstrated complete metabolic response - no FDG-avid disease at the primary site or regional lymph nodes. This was confirmed on flexible nasopharyngoscopy, which showed no residual tumor at the base of tongue.

She presents today approximately 6 months after completion of chemoradiation. She reports a meaningful improvement in quality of life compared to the acute treatment period. She has resumed part-time work as a music teacher, which she notes is gratifying and important to her emotional recovery. She is able to eat a soft mechanical diet and is tolerating thin liquids. She underwent PEG tube removal by gastroenterology approximately one month ago, after demonstrating that she was meeting greater than 85% of her daily caloric needs orally. She endorses persistent dry mouth, which is bothersome particularly while teaching, but reports that the pilocarpine initiated last month has provided some partial relief. She denies new throat pain, new neck lumps, hemoptysis, otalgia, odynophagia at rest, or unexplained weight loss. No fevers, chills, or constitutional symptoms.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: Persistent xerostomia (stable, grade 1-2); mild fatigue with prolonged activity; occasional difficulty with dry or chewy food textures.
Negative: Denies pain at rest in the throat, neck, or ear; no new neck masses; no hemoptysis or blood-tinged sputum; no trismus; no voice change from post-treatment baseline; no nasal obstruction; no visual changes; no dyspnea; no new constitutional symptoms. No nausea, vomiting, or abdominal complaints since PEG tube removal. No lower extremity edema.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vitals: Blood pressure within normal limits. Heart rate regular. Weight: [recorded - net deficit approximately 2 lbs from pre-treatment baseline, continuing to recover]. Performance status: ECOG 0.

General: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and conversant. Mood good.

Head/Neck:
- Oropharynx: Well-healed mucosa. Mild residual erythema of the posterior oropharynx consistent with post-radiation changes. No discrete mass or ulceration visible on direct oral exam. Tongue base not fully visualized without endoscopy - see below.
- Salivary glands: Submandibular glands palpably normal. No parotid masses. Mouth clearly dry on oral inspection consistent with grade 1 xerostomia.
- Dentition: Intact. Tooth extraction site (tooth #19) completely healed.
- Neck: Supple. No palpable cervical, submandibular, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy bilaterally. No palpable masses. Bilateral neck skin with residual hyperpigmentation in prior radiation field; no moist desquamation; no induration suggestive of significant fibrosis.
- Flexible nasopharyngoscopy performed in clinic: Nasal cavity and nasopharynx clear. Oropharynx: mucosa well-healed at the right base of tongue, no visible mass, no ulceration, no abnormal vascular pattern. Bilateral valleculae clear. Supraglottis and glottis unremarkable. Vocal cord mobility normal bilaterally.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. PEG tube site well-healed, no erythema or granulation tissue.
Extremities: No edema. No peripheral neuropathy on gross exam.

---

LABORATORY / DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS
Recent laboratory data reviewed:
- CBC: Within normal limits. No cytopenias.
- BMP: Electrolytes normal. Creatinine 0.85 mg/dL (stable from baseline; no significant nephrotoxicity from cis). eGFR >80 mL/min.
- TSH: 5.8 mIU/L (elevated above normal range). Free T4 low-normal at 0.9 ng/dL. Consistent with radiation-induced hypothyroidism superimposed on pre-existing hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine.

Imaging: Most recent surveillance PET/CT (12 weeks post-treatment) demonstrated complete metabolic response with no FDG-avid disease at the primary site (right base of tongue; previously SUVmax 12.4, now background activity) or at the right level IIA lymph node (previously SUVmax 8.7). No distant metastatic disease. Today no new imaging is being ordered; 12-month post-treatment CT neck with contrast planned per surveillance protocol.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-POSITIVE (p16+) RIGHT BASE OF TONGUE SCC, T2N1M0, STATUS POST DEFINITIVE CONCURRENT CHEMORADIATION - COMPLETE RESPONSE, NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE
Ms. Chen is doing well at the 6-month post-treatment mark. Clinical examination is reassuring: no palpable adenopathy, no endoscopic evidence of recurrent or residual tumor at the primary site. Her 12-week post-treatment PET/CT confirmed complete metabolic response. She is counseled that HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC carries an excellent prognosis, particularly in patients achieving complete response after definitive chemoradiation - 5-year overall survival rates in her population are in the range of 85% or better. No adjuvant systemic therapy is indicated per current standard of care for complete responders.
- Continue NCCN-based HPV-positive OPSCC surveillance: H&P every 1-3 months during years 1-2. Next visit in 3 months.
- CT neck with contrast planned at 12 months post-treatment completion (approximately 6 months from today); PET/CT to be reserved for equivocal findings per clinical judgment.
- Patient counseled to present urgently if new neck mass, persistent throat pain, hemoptysis, otalgia, or unexplained weight loss develop.

2. XEROSTOMIA, GRADE 1 - POST-RADIATION, CHRONIC
Expected late effect of parotid gland irradiation (mean parotid doses: right 28 Gy, left 24 Gy). Patient partially responding to pilocarpine 5 mg TID initiated last visit.
- Continue pilocarpine 5 mg TID; counsel on staying well hydrated. If GI side effects intolerable, can trial dose reduction to 5 mg BID.
- Continue artificial saliva spray (e.g., Biotene) as needed, particularly for teaching and social engagements.
- Reinforce daily fluoride tray use given xerostomia-related increased risk of dental caries. Dental surveillance every 6 months.

3. DYSPHAGIA - IMPROVING, POST-RADIATION
Patient tolerating soft mechanical diet and thin liquids, meeting nutritional needs entirely by mouth since PEG removal. Some difficulty with dry, chewy, or sticky food textures persists.
- Continue outpatient speech-language pathology for dysphagia rehabilitation; goal of advancing to regular diet.
- Encourage Mendelsohn maneuver and supraglottic swallow techniques as instructed by SLP.
- Monitor weight at each visit; goal return to within 1-2 lbs of pre-treatment baseline.

4. RADIATION-INDUCED HYPOTHYROIDISM
TSH now elevated at 5.8 mIU/L with low-normal free T4, on background of pre-existing hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. The thyroid gland sits within the prior radiation field. Radiation thyroiditis/hypothyroidism is a well-recognized late effect.
- Levothyroxine dose increased today (specific dose per pharmacy record). Recheck TSH and free T4 in 6 weeks.
- Long-term thyroid surveillance: TSH annually, or more frequently as needed to maintain euthyroid status.

5. Platinol-RELATED LATE EFFECTS MONITORING
- Nephrotoxicity: Creatinine remains at baseline (0.85 mg/dL); no significant renal impairment. Continue annual BMP monitoring.
- Ototoxicity: Audiogram to be repeated at 12-month visit per institutional protocol (2 cycles of high-dose Platinol). Mild high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss is anticipated but not yet formally documented post-treatment; patient does not endorse subjective hearing complaints at this time.
- Patient counseled on long-term ototoxicity risk in her occupation (music teacher) and to avoid recreational noise exposure without hearing protection.

6. LATE RADIATION EFFECTS - GENERAL COUNSELING
- Carotid stenosis: Counseled that irradiation of the bilateral neck may be associated with carotid atherosclerosis in the long term. Discussed importance of cardiovascular risk factor management (she is a never-smoker, favorable profile). Carotid ultrasound or clinical monitoring per cardiology/vascular surgery referral if symptoms arise.
- Neck skin: Residual post-radiation hyperpigmentation. Reinforce daily broad-spectrum SPF 50 sunscreen for neck and face skin.
- Trismus prevention: No significant trismus at present; encourage jaw-opening exercises as previously instructed.
- Osteoradionecrosis: Low ongoing risk given tooth #19 prophylactic extraction prior to RT. Dental hygiene and early reporting of jaw pain emphasized.

Return to clinic in approximately 3 months for continued surveillance. Patient to follow up with radiation oncology (Dr. Park) and ENT/head and neck surgery per respective schedules. Speech-language pathology and dental oncology follow-up ongoing. Patient understands and is agreeable to the plan.

[Electronically signed by attending medical oncologist]

MEDICAL ONCOLOGY - POST-TREATMENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Head and Neck Oncology Program

ATTENDING: [Medical Oncology, Head and Neck]
VISIT TYPE: Office follow-up, post-treatment surveillance

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Chen is a 52-year-old Chinese-American female, never-smoker, with HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition), presenting for her first scheduled post-treatment follow-up visit approximately 6 weeks after completion of definitive concurrent chemoradiation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen originally presented approximately 9 months ago with a 3-month history of right-sided throat discomfort and mild odynophagia, initially attributed to reflux by her primary care provider. ENT evaluation revealed a right base of tongue mass, biopsy of which confirmed moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with strong p16 positivity (>75% staining) and HPV-16 RNA ISH positivity. Staging workup including CT neck with contrast, PET/CT, and MRI oropharynx/neck confirmed T2N1M0 disease (3.1 cm primary, single 2.4 cm right level IIA nodal metastasis, no extranodal extension, no distant disease). Multidisciplinary tumor board recommended definitive concurrent chemoradiation as standard of care. She completed IMRT to 70 Gy in 35 fractions (SIB technique, bilateral neck elective fields included) with concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 every 3 weeks x2 cycles, finishing treatment approximately 6 weeks ago. Treatment was completed on schedule without interruption.

Her treatment course was complicated by grade 3 mucositis beginning in week 5, necessitating PEG tube placement by gastroenterology for nutritional support. She also experienced grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the bilateral neck, grade 2 fatigue, and grade 1 xerostomia. She successfully completed both planned cycles of cisplatin at full dose without dose reduction. At the end-of-treatment visit (2 weeks ago), she had grade 3 mucositis that was just beginning to improve, was using the PEG tube exclusively for nutrition, and had lost approximately 11 lbs from her pre-treatment baseline.

Today she reports substantial improvement compared with the nadir of her treatment. She rates her throat pain as 2/10 at rest, 4/10 with swallowing - a dramatic improvement from the 8/10 pain she experienced during weeks 5-7 of radiation. She has discontinued all opioid analgesics and is managing with scheduled acetaminophen 1000 mg TID, which she finds adequate. She is working with speech-language pathology and has begun oral trials: she is able to tolerate pureed and soft foods (yogurt, scrambled eggs, mashed potatoes, smoothies) and thin liquids without aspiration per SLP evaluation. She estimates she is getting approximately 40-50% of her daily calories orally and the remainder through the PEG tube with overnight Osmolite 1.5 at 80 mL/hr. COMMENT:
The histomorphology in combination with the immunohistochemical and molecular findings is consistent with HPV-associated (transcriptionally active HPV-16) squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx. The tumor demonstrates the characteristic non-keratinizing to focally keratinizing architecture, pleomorphic nuclei with prominent nucleoli, and basaloid cytology with areas of more abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, consistent with moderately differentiated HPV-positive OPSCC. Strong and diffuse p16 overexpression (>75% of tumor cells, nuclear and cytoplasmic) is confirmed by immunohistochemistry. HPV RNA in situ hybridization (RNAscope) demonstrates punctate and diffuse nuclear and cytoplasmic signal in tumor cells, confirming the presence of transcriptionally active high-risk HPV. These findings are consistent with the clinical and radiographic presentation of a right base of tongue primary tumor.

Lymphovascular invasion is not identified in this biopsy specimen. Perineural invasion is not identified. Tumor margin assessment is not applicable to this biopsy specimen.

GROSS DESCRIPTION:

Specimen A is received fresh in a container labeled "right base of tongue biopsy." The specimen consists of multiple tan-pink, somewhat friable, irregular soft tissue fragments. In aggregate, the fragments measure approximately 1.2 x 0.8 x 0.4 cm. The tissue is entirely submitted for histologic examination in two cassettes (A1-A2). MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Histologic sections of the right base of tongue biopsy reveal fragments of respiratory-type and stratified squamous epithelium overlying a neoplastic proliferation. The tumor demonstrates an invasive growth pattern with predominantly nested and sheet-like architectural arrangements. Individual tumor cell nests are surrounded by a desmoplastic fibrous stroma with scattered inflammatory infiltrate including lymphocytes and plasma cells.

Tumor cells are polygonal to elongated, with moderate to abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm. Nuclei are enlarged, vesicular, and pleomorphic with prominent single or multiple nucleoli. Mitotic figures are readily identified, including atypical forms. Focal intercellular bridges and small foci of individual cell keratinization are present, though overt keratin pearl formation is not conspicuous. A subset of cells in the periphery of nests displays a basaloid morphology with higher nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios, consistent with the spectrum of moderately differentiated HPV-positive SCC.

The overlying surface squamous epithelium shows focal dysplastic changes at the epithelial-stromal interface.

Lymphovascular spaces: No convincing lymphovascular invasion identified on H&E-stained sections.
Perineural invasion: Not identified.
Necrosis: Focal comedonecrosis within tumor nests, not extensive.
Margins: Not applicable (incisional biopsy specimen).

It retains homogeneous enhancement without internal necrosis, irregularity, or concerning morphologic features. This continued decrease in size is consistent with ongoing post-treatment involution and fibrosis rather than any active nodal process. No other enlarged or morphologically suspicious lymph nodes are identified in levels I through V on the right.

Left neck: No pathologically enlarged or morphologically suspicious lymph nodes are identified. Several subcentimeter lymph nodes are present in levels II and III, all with preserved fatty hilum and benign morphology.

No bilateral level I, level IV, or level V adenopathy. No retropharyngeal or parapharyngeal lymph nodes of concern.

Salivary Glands:
Bilateral parotid glands show mild volume reduction and heterogeneous fatty replacement relative to the prior study, likely progressive radiation-related parenchymal atrophy — a recognized and expected late sequela of head and neck irradiation. The submandibular glands are similarly mildly atrophic. No discrete mass or obstructive ductal pathology is identified within any of the major salivary glands.

Thyroid Gland:
The thyroid gland is slightly heterogeneous in echotexture with mild parenchymal volume reduction, findings potentially attributable in part to prior low-dose radiation scatter from the inferior neck fields. No suspicious focal nodule, calcification, or dominant mass is identified. These findings may warrant correlation with thyroid function tests given the patient's known history of hypothyroidism and prior radiation exposure.

Soft Tissues of the Neck:
Diffuse subcutaneous fat stranding and mild skin thickening of the bilateral anterior and lateral neck persist, consistent with post-radiation changes to the integument and superficial soft tissues. Slight increase in muscular density of the bilateral sternocleidomastoid and strap muscles is present, again consistent with radiation fibrosis and unchanged from the prior examination. The carotid arteries and jugular veins are patent bilaterally without evidence of occlusion, significant stenosis, or interval development of new vascular disease. No soft tissue mass or abnormal enhancement outside the known treatment-related changes.

Oral Cavity:
No new mass or concerning lesion within the oral cavity, floor of mouth, or oral tongue. Postsurgical absence of the lower left first molar (tooth #19) noted, consistent with prior pre-radiation extraction. Mandible without periosteal reaction, cortical destruction, or features to suggest osteoradionecrosis.

Skull Base and Cervical Spine:
No osseous erosion or destructive lesion involving the skull base, clivus, or cervical vertebral bodies. No paravertebral soft tissue mass. Degenerative endplate changes in the mid and lower cervical spine are an incidental finding.

Chest (Scout and Upper-Thoracic Cuts):
Lungs are grossly clear at the lung apices visualized on the inferior-most images. No apical pulmonary nodule or pleural abnormality identified on the limited views available. No supraclavicular adenopathy.

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of recurrent or residual primary tumor at the right base of tongue. The post-radiation soft tissue changes in this region are stable in comparison to the prior study and do not harbor any suspicious focal lesion.

2. Continued decrease in size of the previously involved right level IIA lymph node (now approximately 0.6 cm, down from 0.8 cm at 12-month follow-up and 2.4 cm at initial diagnosis), consistent with ongoing post-treatment involution. No evidence of nodal recurrence.

3. No new cervical lymphadenopathy. No new soft tissue mass in the neck.

4. Bilateral parotid and submandibular gland volume loss consistent with expected radiation-related parenchymal atrophy; progressed mildly since prior study but within the expected trajectory for this treatment history.

5. Residual diffuse post-radiation soft tissue and integumentary changes in the neck, stable and expected at this interval.

6. Mild heterogeneity and volume reduction of the thyroid gland; recommend clinical correlation with thyroid function studies.

Overall, the findings are reassuring at 18-month post-treatment surveillance with no imaging evidence of locoregional recurrence or new disease. Post-radiation sequelae continue to evolve in a stable and expected fashion.

PET/CT SCAN WITH FDG — WHOLE BODY

EXAMINATION: FDG-PET/CT, skull base through mid-thigh (whole body staging protocol)
INDICATION: 52-year-old female with biopsy-proven HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, T2N1M0 by prior CT neck. Presenting for whole-body staging prior to initiation of definitive therapy.
COMPARISON: CT neck with contrast obtained recently (reviewed for correlation).

TECHNIQUE:
Patient fasted for greater than 6 hours prior to examination. Serum glucose at time of injection was 88 mg/dL. 10.2 mCi of F-18 FDG was administered intravenously. Standard uptake period of approximately 60 minutes followed prior to image acquisition. Whole body PET images were acquired from skull base to mid-thigh with concurrent low-dose CT for attenuation correction and anatomic localization. No IV contrast administered with the CT portion of this examination. Images were reconstructed using iterative reconstruction algorithm and reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes as well as fused PET/CT images. Maximum intensity projection (MIP) images reviewed.

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK — PRIMARY TUMOR SITE:
There is a conspicuous focus of markedly increased FDG uptake in the right base of tongue corresponding to the primary tumor site. The hypermetabolic lesion measures approximately 3.1 cm in its greatest transaxial dimension on co-registered CT, with a peak SUVmax of 12.4. The uptake is heterogeneous, with the most avid component centered at the right posterolateral base of tongue extending toward the right glossoepiglottic fold. --- TIER 1 / POTENTIALLY ACTIONABLE ALTERATIONS ---

1. PIK3CA — p.E545K (c.1633G>A)
   Gene Function:  Phosphatidylinositol-4,5-bisphosphate 3-kinase catalytic subunit alpha; PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway
   Variant Class:  Missense, Exon 9 (helical domain)
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 28%
   Read Support:   Alt/Total: 143/511
   Predicted Effect: Activating — E545K is a well-characterized hotspot mutation in the helical domain of PIK3CA. Disrupts inhibitory interaction with the regulatory p85 subunit (PIK3R1), leading to constitutive PI3K signaling and downstream AKT/mTOR activation independent of RTK input.
   Clinical Significance: PIK3CA E545K is among the most frequently observed activating mutations in HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC (reported in 15–25% of cases), consistent with the TP53-wild-type/HPV-driven carcinogenetic background of this tumor. This variant is associated with sensitivity to PI3K pathway inhibitors (e.g., alpelisib, copanlisib) and mTOR inhibitors in selected tumor types, though clinical evidence in OPSCC is investigational. No approved PI3K-targeted therapy exists for OPSCC at this time; enrollment in clinical trials targeting the PI3K/mTOR pathway may be appropriate.

FAT1 inactivation is common in head and neck SCC (reported in up to 30% of cases), with functional studies suggesting roles in Hippo/YAP1 pathway dysregulation and Wnt pathway crosstalk. GENOMIC SUMMARY AND INTERPRETIVE COMMENT
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
This tumor demonstrates a genomic profile highly consistent with HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. Hallmark features include:

  (1) TP53 wild-type status — reflecting HPV E6-mediated p53 protein degradation rather than somatic mutation, a defining feature of HPV-driven OPSCC
  (2) Intact CDKN2A — p16 overexpression in this tumor is a consequence of Rb inactivation by HPV E7, not genomic CDKN2A loss
  (3) Low tumor mutational burden (5 mut/Mb) — characteristic of virally-driven rather than carcinogen-driven tumorigenesis
  (4) PIK3CA E545K activating mutation — the PI3K pathway is among the most commonly altered in HPV-positive OPSCC; this helical domain hotspot mutation is of potential therapeutic interest
  (5) Chromatin remodeling gene alterations (KMT2D frameshift, EP300 missense) — frequently co-occurring in HNSCC, reflecting epigenetic dysregulation
  (6) NOTCH1 and FAT1 loss-of-function mutations — consistent with the known tumor suppressor roles of these genes in squamous epithelium
  (7) Low-level copy gains in CCND1 (11q13), FGFR1 (8p11), and EGFR polysomy — collectively common in HNSCC; no high-level amplifications
  (8) Confirmed HPV-16 genomic integration — supporting active viral oncogenesis
  (9) PD-L1 CPS 12 — intermediate positive, potentially relevant in recurrent/metastatic setting

No tier 1 FDA-approved targeted therapies are currently indicated for this patient in the frontline locally advanced setting based on the identified genomic alterations. The PIK3CA E545K mutation represents the most clinically relevant finding for potential future therapeutic targeting, particularly if systemic therapy becomes indicated in recurrent or metastatic disease.

This genomic profile does not alter the standard-of-care recommendation for definitive concurrent chemoradiation (cisplatin-based) for stage III HPV-positive OPSCC, which remains the evidence-based treatment approach.

Clinical trial considerations: Trials evaluating PI3K/mTOR pathway inhibitors in HNSCC, trials incorporating checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1/PD-L1) in combination with definitive chemoradiation, and epigenetic targeting strategies may be relevant for this patient in appropriate disease contexts.

METHODS
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
DNA was extracted from macro-dissected FFPE tumor tissue with confirmed ≥20% tumor nuclei. Library preparation was performed using a hybridization capture-based approach (523 cancer-relevant genes, including full exon coverage of all listed genes plus selected introns for fusion detection). Sequencing was performed on Illumina NovaSeq 6000. Bioinformatic analysis included alignment to hg19, variant calling using validated somatic variant callers with matched normal (patient PBMC-derived germline comparator), copy number analysis using an in-house panel of normals baseline, and fusion detection from RNA-seq data. Variants with VAF <5% or read depth <50x were excluded from reporting. Germline filtering applied; variants with >1% population allele frequency in gnomAD were classified as likely germline and not reported as somatic unless known oncogenic hotspots. MSI analysis performed using 114 microsatellite loci. TMB calculated from exonic somatic mutations per megabase of sequenced coding genome. PD-L1 IHC (Dako 22C3 pharmDx) performed independently by anatomic pathology on a representative FFPE section.

Assay Sensitivity: VAF ≥5% with ≥50x coverage for SNVs/indels; copy number changes ≥1.5-fold; fusions supported by ≥5 spanning reads.

- Baseline labs: Cr 0.8 mg/dL, GFR est. 88 mL/min, CBC within normal limits, LFTs normal, albumin 4.1 g/dL.

At the time of today's presentation, patient denies frank trismus, airway compromise, significant weight loss, or hemoptysis. She reports ongoing mild-to-moderate throat discomfort and mild difficulty swallowing bulky solids. She is eating and drinking without significant restriction at present. Performance status ECOG 0.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Hypothyroidism - managed with levothyroxine, TSH last checked 6 months ago, within normal limits
2. Seasonal allergic rhinitis - uses loratadine as needed
3. No prior head and neck surgery
4. No prior radiation therapy
5. No prior chemotherapy
6. No significant cardiac, pulmonary, or renal history

---

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Never-smoker. Denies significant alcohol use (occasional glass of wine socially). No illicit drug use. Works as a high school music teacher - an active and demanding professional role requiring significant vocal and communicative capacity. Married. Two adult children. Lives at home with spouse. Able to drive and manage all ADLs independently. Strong social support system. Motivated and engaged in her own care.

---

FAMILY HISTORY:
No family history of head and neck cancer. No family history of oropharyngeal or oral cavity malignancies. Mother had breast cancer at age 68 (alive, in remission). Father deceased from myocardial infarction. No other known hereditary cancer syndromes.

---

ALLERGIES:
NKDA (No Known Drug Allergies)

---

MEDICATIONS:
1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (hypothyroidism)
2. Loratadine 10 mg PO daily as needed (seasonal allergic rhinitis)
3. Multivitamin daily

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: right-sided throat discomfort (moderate, ongoing), mild odynophagia with solids, occasional right-sided referred otalgia, mild fatigue.
Negative: No fever, chills, or night sweats. No significant weight loss (reports < 5 lbs over the past 3 months, attributed to decreased appetite). No frank dysphagia to liquids. No trismus or limited jaw opening. No hemoptysis. No shortness of breath. No airway symptoms. No new neck masses noted by patient. No vision changes, facial numbness, or tongue deviation. No bone pain. No neurologic symptoms.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 118/74, HR 72, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, Temp 98.4F. Height 162 cm, Weight 61 kg, BSA 1.65 m2.
General: Pleasant, well-appearing female in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x4. Excellent communicator; no dysarthria or voice change at this time.
HEENT: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No facial asymmetry. Mouth opening approximately 4 cm (normal), no trismus. Dentition: multiple amalgam restorations; tooth #19 (lower left first molar) with visible periapical disease on exam, flagged for dental oncology. Oral mucosa without lesions. Tongue mobile, no deviation. Oropharynx: flexible nasopharyngoscopy (performed by Dr. Yee): right base of tongue exophytic mass approximately 3 cm, mucosal surface friable, does not cross midline. Epiglottis and vallecula clear. Hypopharynx normal appearance. No airway compromise.
Neck: Right level IIA: 2.5 cm firm, slightly mobile lymph node, non-tender. No other palpable adenopathy bilaterally. No sternocleidomastoid or trapezius tenderness. No thyroid mass.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended.
Extremities: No edema.
Neurologic: No focal deficits.

---

LAB RESULTS:
CBC: WBC 7.2, Hgb 13.6, Plt 241 - within normal limits.
CMP: Na 139, K 4.0, Cr 0.8, BUN 14, GFR est. 88, glucose 92 - within normal limits.
LFTs: AST 22, ALT 18, AlkPhos 74, total bili 0.6 - normal.
Albumin: 4.1 g/dL.
TSH: 2.4 mIU/L (within normal limits on levothyroxine).
Baseline audiogram: Normal hearing bilaterally. No high-frequency deficit.

---

IMAGING REVIEWED AT MDT:
CT neck with contrast, PET/CT whole body, and MRI oropharynx/neck all reviewed by the multidisciplinary team (see formal reports). Consensus: T2N1M0, right base of tongue HPV-positive OPSCC, locoregional disease only. No distant metastases. No radiographic extranodal extension. No involvement beyond oropharynx.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

52-year-old never-smoking Chinese-American woman with newly diagnosed p16-positive (HPV-16), T2N1M0 Stage III squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue. ECOG PS 0. Excellent nutritional and functional status at presentation. No adverse pathologic features (no ENE, no LVI, no perineural invasion on biopsy).

MDT DISCUSSION SUMMARY:

1. TREATMENT APPROACH - DEFINITIVE CONCURRENT CHEMORADIATION:
The MDT consensus recommendation is definitive concurrent chemoradiation (CRT) as the primary treatment modality. This is the established standard of care for node-positive, locally advanced, HPV-positive OPSCC. Primary surgical resection (TORS + neck dissection) was discussed; while technically feasible, the node-positive disease (N1) would mandate adjuvant treatment regardless, and the overall cure rates with definitive CRT are equivalent with avoidance of surgical morbidity. TORS approach declined by tumor board.

2. RADIATION THERAPY:
Dr. Park to plan IMRT using simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique. PTV_High (primary and involved node): 70 Gy / 35 fractions. Elective nodal volumes: 59.5 Gy and 56 Gy per SIB plan. MRI-based tumor volume delineation already completed. Daily CBCT image guidance planned. Simulation to be scheduled within the next 10 days; treatment to begin approximately 10 days after simulation.

3. SYSTEMIC THERAPY:
Cisplatin 100 mg/m2 IV q3 weeks x2 cycles concurrent with radiation. Patient has excellent renal function (GFR 88), normal baseline hearing, and no contraindications to cisplatin. BSA 1.65 m2; actual dose approximately 165 mg per cycle. Aggressive antiemetic prophylaxis (ondansetron, dexamethasone) and IV hydration to be administered with each cycle. Ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity monitoring planned throughout.

   NOTE: De-escalation trial enrollment discussed. Patient does not meet criteria for currently enrolling institutional trial (no active ECOG protocol open at this time for this presentation). Standard-dose treatment (70 Gy + cisplatin) to proceed.

4. DENTAL ONCOLOGY (Dr. Nakamura):
Pre-radiation dental evaluation completed today. Tooth #19 (lower left first molar) has significant periapical disease with risk of osteoradionecrosis if radiation delivered with infected tooth in situ. Extraction of tooth #19 by oral and maxillofacial surgery recommended prior to radiation start. Custom oral stent/tongue depressor device for radiation positioning to be fabricated concurrently. Comprehensive fluoride tray program and dental hygiene instructions initiated. Patient instructed to maintain meticulous oral hygiene throughout treatment.

5. SPEECH-LANGUAGE PATHOLOGY (J. Morales):
Pre-treatment swallowing evaluation performed. Current swallowing function intact; mild dysphagia to dry/bulky solids noted but liquids and soft foods without difficulty. Baseline modified barium swallow study deferred given intact current function; to be repeated post-treatment as indicated. Prophylactic swallowing exercises initiated. Patient counseled on anticipated acute mucositis and dysphagia during treatment.

6. NUTRITION/DIETETICS (M. Owens):
Baseline nutritional status adequate (albumin 4.1, no significant weight loss). High-calorie oral supplements initiated (e.g., Ensure Plus BID). Patient and family counseled about anticipated significant dysphagia and weight loss during weeks 3-6 of treatment. Prophylactic PEG tube placement discussed in detail; patient opts to defer PEG placement at this time and pursue reactive/as-needed placement should oral intake become inadequate. Close monitoring plan established with threshold weight loss / caloric intake triggers for PEG discussion.

7. SUPPORTIVE CARE:
- Pain management: Prescribing clinicians aware of anticipated significant oropharyngeal mucositis. Acetaminophen scheduled + opioid prescription as bridge discussed with patient in anticipation of week 3-4 symptom escalation.
- Oral hygiene: Magic mouthwash prescription, saline/baking soda rinses.
- Skin care: Aquaphor ointment for neck skin during radiation.

8. FOLLOW-UP:
Post-dental extraction wound healing to be confirmed at approximately 14-day follow-up prior to radiation start. Weekly on-treatment assessments planned during CRT, alternating between radiation oncology and medical oncology. Post-treatment response assessment imaging (PET/CT preferred) planned at approximately 12 weeks following treatment completion per institutional and NCCN guidelines.

Patient and her spouse were present for MDT discussion. Treatment plan reviewed in detail. Risks and benefits discussed including acute toxicities (mucositis, dysphagia, xerostomia, dermatitis, nausea/emesis, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, fatigue), late effects (xerostomia, dysphagia, trismus, hypothyroidism, carotid stenosis, osteoradionecrosis), and expected oncologic outcomes. Patient verbalized understanding and elected to proceed with the recommended concurrent CRT approach. She has no questions at this time and expresses readiness to begin treatment.

Electronically signed,
Robert Lin, MD
Head and Neck Medical Oncology

Documented with input from all MDT co-attendees listed above.
